title: How to create safe lip results using the 4mm needle technique.
filename: How to create safe lip results using the 4mm needle technique..en.vtt
excerpt: Why don't I inject like this clinicians quite often send me clips from around the internet and ask me is this the way that I would do it and the answer when I looked at this clip is no and there are some important but very subtle reasons why because I do a very similar injection …

segments.text: why don't I inject like this clinicians quite often send me clips from around the internet and ask me is this the way that I would do it and the answer when I looked at this clip is no and there are some important but very subtle reasons why because I do a very similar injection technique but let me use this as a teaching point to help you understand my mental model so that you can inject more safely and if you're doing similar injection techniques increase your resolution of understanding of both the anatomy and the risks with different injection types so this technique is the 4mm needle technique this is the volumizing component of this where we're injecting filler directly into the lip body and trying to add a roundness or a fullness to the middle third of the lip so let me explain what I see in this clip and why I don't like it the first thing is the angle of Entry so this angle here relative to the facial plane you can see that it's a relatively low angle which means we're pointing upwards his syringe bevel or her syringe bevel is nearly resting on the chin and it means that this angle of Entry is like this now the

other aspect which is relevant is that the point of entry is relatively high in the lip it's about the top 2/3 of the lip so it's this combination of an entry point like this with an entry point that's relatively close to where I believe the archery will be which we'll have a look at in 3D shortly that I think makes this the perfect angle to just about prick the superior label artery while you're injecting here it is from a different angle relatively low angle of Entry entry point about a third of the way down the lip and we're pointing up like this obviously this is a longer needle than in the clip but the principle applies where is it pointing to if you keep going where are you likely to hit it's directly behind the orbicularis orus muscle straight into the path of the superior label artery the angle of Entry is if I try and emulate it it's more like this so it's this angle here so let's have a look at this in a 3D render of the anatomy of the lip which is done to scale where the positions of the labial

arteries are accurate to the millimeter which is very important because we're using this technique with a 4mm needle now the entry point of the 4mm needle in this example the way I'm perceiving it based on this clip is that we're pointing down we're resting almost on the lower lip pointing upwards and if you have a look what's directly behind our bolus which is the one we're trying to do is we are directly behind um we're directly inferior to the superior label artery you can see this artery here this is the one that I'm worried about hitting particularly when you're doing a Bolis and it's this relative angle of Entry that makes the artery directly behind where the filler is so if you imagine this needle pointing upwards you can see that the artery is directly behind that area now if you were to inject at a different angle you would miss that artery so the question in your mind should always be am I aiming towards the under surface of the orbicularis orus and if that is the

case you are where the artery is in 85% of cases essentially that inferior approach lines up the needle the filler where you want it but the artery is just behind it there's one other thing to think about as well which I also see happening in this video which is firstly as the needle goes in there's some compression of the tissue so if you're compressing tissue the 4 mm safety margin is less effective because you're effectively making that needle act as if it's a longer needle because you're compressing tissue which is quite an easy thing to do so maybe it's reaching 5 mm in which is exactly the depth of the artery the next thing is there's no aspirating going on in this video the 4mm needle is actually a really good needle to aspirate with it makes aspiration extremely sensitive and if you're going to do a bolus injection why not because there's clearly no movement going on there no other safety things I can detect um so why are we not just adding that additional step of a little aspiration and remember if you aspirate you have a negative pressure in your syringe and then movement increases the

sensitivity of your aspirating because as you're moving you're scanning more of an area and you will see this when you pay attention to aspirating I've got several clips of it myself where the movement draws the tip of your needle back into a vessel which you've already gone through and then you get a positive flashback before you start injecting so a little bit of movement aspirating changing your angle this injection could be a lot safer for exactly this same result so let me show you how I would improve upon this injection technique the first thing is my entry point would not be so Superior it wouldn't be up here it would be a little bit lower now this looks like nothing but 2 mm and that that's relevant especially when I now do the next thing which is change the angle so if now I'm injecting more at this angle I should be passing underneath most of those arteries I'm directly towards the body of the lip but not pointing underneath orbicular asaurus once now this is not my normal injection angle but I'm not resting my hands on the patient anywhere but if you've then found your depth this needle is longer than a 4mm needle technique you can then aspirate now this is the

bit that I'd like people to think about more which is if you do a little bit of movement at this stage you're basically moving in and out of where that whole bolus will go and you're increasing your sensitivity to getting a positive aspiration which would tell you to not inject obviously we know that a negative doesn't mean you're definitely not in a vessel this is one way of increasing your ability to detect intravascular insertion point which is this little bit of movement is fine and then you can start injecting your small bolus of 015 or 01 if you want to make it a little bit safer you could change angle and repeat that process a little bit so just I've just move slightly lateral not Superior so it's a slightly different point for another little injection and that way you could put 01 in with multiple safety steps pointed in a overall safer angle of Entry so every time you see someone injecting and it's different to how you're currently injecting the worst case scenario is that you immediately switch and you do it their way because you think they are

more experienced or better than you in some way but you don't actually understand what's going on underneath the tissue so the real lesson here for me is not that you copy my technique or that you change technique it's that you understand more what I'm imagining and then you can make better sense when you see other injectors of what they are imagining and then you try and pick out something that makes the most possible sense to you given all the learning that you've done it really isn't about technique versus technique it's more about mental model resolution and I hope this video has helped you develop yours so that you can inject with more confidence and more safety and get better results with your patients if you love learning Anatomy like this and want to be even more Hands-On and get really good at this really important foundation subject I am launching as part of my new membership a 3D Anatomy experience now the doors are currently closed but you can sign up to the waiting list to make sure you're the first to know when it goes live so make sure you click on the link that comes with this [Music]
